coredata.dc:title: A functional carbon dots induce ferroptosis by suppressing PLPP4 activity to inhibit glioblastoma growth
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Kun
Zhang, Lianglin
Gao, Wei
Lin, Xiang
Long, Xinmiao
Wang, Yang
Wu, Minghua
coredata.dc:description: Glioblastoma is a fatal primary brain cancer lacking effective therapeutic drugs. The presence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) severely hinders the delivery of drugs to the brain. In recent years, nanoparticles, especially carbon dots (CDs), are promising drug delivery strategy for CNS diseases. Here, we synthesized a novel carbon dots（MGA-CDs）for glioblastoma treatment by hydrothermal method using metformin and gallic acid as precursor. MGA-CDs shows dominant BBB permeability and sensitive anti-tumor activity. In addition, MGA-CDs exhibits significant capability of targeting tumor cells mitochondria without the aid of anyextra targeting molecules, resulting in the shrunken of mitochondria and reduced numbers of mitochondrial cristae. Transcriptome profiling suggested that MGA-CDs disturbs the glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway by inhibiting the expression of PLPP4, thereby inducing ferroptosis. The efficient therapeutic potency of MGA-CDs is further confirmed in human-derived orthotopic glioblastoma mice model. MGA-CDs significantly inhibited the growth of intracranial tumors and prolonged the survival of tumor-bearing mice. This work presents a viable strategy that development of CDs-based novel therapeutic agent for glioblastoma.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The cytotoxicity and BBB permeability of MGA-CDs. (a) Schematic representation of the one-pot synthesis of MGA-CDs based on metformin and gallic acid. (b-d) Viability rate of cells treated with MGA-CDs (b), MCA-CDs (c) and TMZ (d) at different concentrations for 24 h. (e) IC50 Value of MGA-CDs, MCA-CDs, and TMZ on U251 and U87 cells. (f-g) EdU positive cell quantity on U251 and U87 cells treatment with MGA-CDs after 24 h (f). The activities of U251 and U87 cells treated with MGA-CDs at different time gradients (g). The concentrations of MGA-CDs both correspond to the IC50 values. (h-i) MGA-CDs decreased wound healing rate (h) and invasive cell number (i) in glioblastoma cells. (j) Schematic diagram of the in vitro BBB transwell model setup and experimentation. (k) TEER values of the bEnd.3 cell monolayer before and after treatment with MGA-CDs and TMZ. (l) Penetration capability of MGA-CDs crossing the vitro BBB transwell model after 4 h of incubation. Data are presented as means ± SD.

Characterization of MGA-CDs morphology and optical properties. (a) Schematic representation of MGA-CDs' BBB penetration and targeting of mitochondria. (b) TEM image of as-prepared MGA-CDs. (c) The size distribution of MGA-CDs was estimated by counting 50 particles. (d) PL spectra of MGA-CDs in aqueous solutions (0.02 mg mL-1). Inset shows a photograph of MGA-CDs in aqueous solution under UV light (385 nm). (e) Excitation-dependent PL of MGA-CDs. (f) UV–vis spectra of MGA-CDs, gallic acid and metformin. (g) FT-IR spectrum of MGA-CDs.

The structure and surface potential of MGA-CDs. (a) XRD pattern of MGA-CDs. (b) XPS survey spectra. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (c), N1s (d), and O1s (e) of MGA-CDs. (f) Zeta-potential of MGA-CDs in the aqueous solution. (g) 1H NMR spectrum of MGA-CDs in D2O.

MGA-CDs targets mitochondria and induces ferroptosis. (a) Immunofluorescence detection showed the aggregation of MGA-CDs in mitochondria (yellow). (b) Transmission electron microscopy showed that the shrunken mitochondria and the reduced numbers of mitochondrial crista in tumor cells after treatment with MGA-CDs. (c) MGA-CDs increases the LPO in glioblastoma cells. (d) MGA-CDs increases the levels of divalent iron ions in glioblastoma cells. (e) Ferroptosis markers expression in glioblastoma cells treated with MGA-CDs were examined by Western blotting. GAPDH was used as loading control. MGA-CDs down-regulated the ferroptosis suppressor genes GPX4 and SLC7A11, while increased expression of ferroptosis driver genes COX2 and TfR. LPO: lipid peroxidation rate. Data are presented as means ± SD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

MGA-CDs targets PLPP4 to induce glycerophospholipid metabolism disorder. (a) Waterfall plot of the enriched pathways from the KEGG comparing MGA-CDs treated and untreated U251 cells. lipid metabolism related pathways are indicated in red. (b) Heatmap depicting the differential expression of lipid metabolism related genes between U251 cells treated with MGA-CDs and without. Normalized log2 fold change. (c) Alteration frequency of the lipid metabolism related differential genes of U251 cells treated with MGA-CDs in TCGA glioblastoma patient samples. (d) QRT-PCR validation of lipid Metabolism-related differential genes. (e) Western blotting assays showed MGA-CDs down-regulated PLPP4. (f-g) MGA-CDs decreases PL content (f) and increases PA content (g) in U251 cell. PA: phosphatidic acid; PL: phospholipid. Data are presented as means ± SD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Downregulation of PLPP4 promotes ferroptosis. (a) QRT-PCR verify the knockdown efficiency of ShPLPP4. (b) Under treatment with Erastin, ferroptosis markers expression following PLPP4 downregulation was detected by Western blotting. (c-d) Under treatment with Erastin, down-regulation of PLPP4 expression increases the LPO (c) and the levels of divalent iron ions (d) in glioblastoma cells. LPO: lipid peroxidation rate. (e) PLPP4 is decreased when cells were treated by Erastin in glioblastoma cells. Data are presented as means ± SD.

MGA-CDs boosted cancer treatment in orthotopic glioblastoma model. (a) Schematic illustration of MGA-CDs therapeutic schedule in human-derived orthotopic malignant glioblastoma model. (b) Bioluminescence images of the luciferase-labelled U251 cell orthotopic glioblastoma-bearing mice collected at day 0, 8, 16 post treatment with PBS (300 μl), TMZ (400 μg/ml, 300 μl) and MGA-CDs (400 μg/ml, 300 μl). (c) Quantitation of the fluorescence intensity of bioluminescence images (n = 5 mice per group). (d) Ex vivo fluorescence images of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and Brain) collected from orthotopic glioblastoma-bearing mice injected with MGA-CDs (400 μg/ml, 300 μl) at 2 h post tail vein injection. (e) H&E staining and Ki-67, COX2 and TfR expression of GBM tissues from tumor-bearing mice treated with PBS and MGA-CDs. (f) RT-PCR validation of PLPP4 from xenograft tumor. Data are presented as means ± SD.

head.ce:title: A functional carbon dots induce ferroptosis by suppressing PLPP4 activity to inhibit glioblastoma growth

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glioblastoma is a fatal primary brain cancer lacking effective therapeutic drugs. The presence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) severely hinders the delivery of drugs to the brain. In recent years, nanoparticles, especially carbon dots (CDs), are promising drug delivery strategy for CNS diseases. Here, we synthesized a novel carbon dots（MGA-CDs）for glioblastoma treatment by hydrothermal method using metformin and gallic acid as precursor. MGA-CDs shows dominant BBB permeability and sensitive anti-tumor activity. In addition, MGA-CDs exhibits significant capability of targeting tumor cells mitochondria without the aid of anyextra targeting molecules, resulting in the shrunken of mitochondria and reduced numbers of mitochondrial cristae. Transcriptome profiling suggested that MGA-CDs disturbs the glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway by inhibiting the expression of PLPP4, thereby inducing ferroptosis. The efficient therapeutic potency of MGA-CDs is further confirmed in human-derived orthotopic glioblastoma mice model. MGA-CDs significantly inhibited the growth of intracranial tumors and prolonged the survival of tumor-bearing mice. This work presents a viable strategy that development of CDs-based novel therapeutic agent for glioblastoma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioblastoma is the most common and aggressive form of brain cancer, accounting for nearly 48 % of primary central nervous system (CNS) malignant tumors. The prognosis for newly diagnosed glioblastoma remains dire despite years of intensive basic, translational, and clinical research, the median survival of glioblastoma patients is only 14 months after standard of care surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy [1]. It is almost impossible for complete surgical ablation to treat glioblastoma due to its high invasiveness. The efficacy of radiotherapy is usually limited by the intrinsic radio-resistance and the hypoxic tumor microenvironment (TME) [2]. The greatest challenge in chemotherapy of glioblastoma arises from drug delivery. The existence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) hinders drugs delivery to the brain [3]. BBB represents a physical and biochemical barrier to provide a sanctuary for the brain by hindering 90 % of small molecules and almost 100 % of large molecules [4]. Over the past 30 years, only 4 chemotherapeutic agents were FDA-approved for the clinical treatment of glioblastoma, including lomustine, carmustine, temozolomide (TMZ) and bevacizumab. However, these chemotherapeutic agents did not significantly improve the prognosis of glioblastoma patients, 90 % of glioblastoma patients invariably recurred. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new drug delivery strategies to bypass BBB for glioblastoma treatment.

Nowadays, the local delivery is the most clinically common strategy for drug delivery to the brain, such as Gliadels wafers and convection-enhanced diffusion. The local delivery strategy successfully circumvented the BBB and achieved high local drug concentration at tumor bed. However, this approach is highly aggressive with the adverse events of confusion, convulsions, infection, intracranial hypertension, brain edema etc [5,6]. With the in-depth understanding in molecular biology of the BBB, it is urgent to develop novel drug delivery strategies. Some biological, chemical or physical factors can disrupt the integrity of BBB to promote the uptake and penetration of drug into the brain [7]. What should be paid attention to is that the disruption of BBB cut both ways. The disrupted BBB not only enhances permeability of drugs, but also allows harmful toxins in the blood into the brain causing neurotoxicity. Thus, an ideal method to increase BBB permeability should be based on safeguard of blood–brain barrier integrity.

With the rapid progression of nanomedicine, nanoparticle-based applications in drug formulations and delivery have emerged as the most utilized nanomedicine system [8]. The nanoparticle can be modified by cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs) or BBB-targeting ligands to effectively penetrate BBB for drug delivery and intracranial tumor imaging [9]. Although some special biomaterials (e.g., plasma membrane) and complex surface modifications (e.g., densely PEG-coated) can greatly reduce the biological toxicity of nanocarriers [10,11], a large number of nanocarriers gathered in the brain are likely to induce some serious mental diseases, such as epilepsy and abscess. Therefore, the intracranial drug delivery nanocarriers must have high biological affinity and degradability.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, was first put forward by Xu in 2004 [12]. It has been intensively intrigued and studied owing to its feature of negligible toxicity [13], photoluminescence [14] and low-cost production [15]. CDs can be formed after a series of dehydration and decarboxylation reactions of hydroxyl(–OH)-rich monomer molecules and another amino(–NH2)-rich monomer by hydrothermal method [16]. These CDs are not only less than 10 nm in diameter but also endow some advantageous surface chemical properties such as amphiphilicity and low charge, which enables CDs to cross the BBB and facilitates cells to uptake them via endocytosis [16]. CDs has become an ideal candidate in CNS disease diagnosis and treatment [17]. Thus, we propose a new strategy to develop novel therapeutic CDs via enhancement of their BBB permeability and clinical availability brain cancer treatment.

Here, we synthesized a new CDs (MGA-CDs) by hydrothermal method to catalyze the dehydration and decarboxylation between metformin and Gallic acid. We investigated the material characteristics of MGA-CDs, including particle size, FTIR spectrum, element composition, surface active group and zeta potential. The biological characteristics of MGA-CDs have been verified. MGA-CDs can freely penetrate the BBB and induce glioblastoma cells ferroptosis by targeting mitochondria to inhibit PLPP4 activity related to glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway. Importantly, we reported for the first time that PLPP4 may be a novel inhibitor of ferroptosis. Moreover, mice bearing glioblastoma xenografts treatment with MGA-CDs showed reduced tumor volume. This study highlights a promising candidate for therapeutic use against glioblastoma.

Gallic acid, sodium hydroxide and metformin hydrochloride were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water used in this experiment was purified via the WP-UP-YJ-20 water purification system. MGA-CDs were synthesized using a simple one pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 5 g of gallic acid powder and 2 g of metformin hydrochloride were mixed with sodium hydroxide (1.2 g) and water (70 ml) in a 250 ml autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h to obtain a brown residue. After cooling to room temperature, large particles in the solution were removed through centrifugation and filtering. The MGA-CDs were further purified by dialyzing and then stored at 4 °C, which can be re-dispersed in redistilled water for further characterization.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements used a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi surface analysis system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, D8 ADVANCE). The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the MGA-CDs were recorded on a GENESYS 180 spectrophotometer and F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a IRAffinity (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrometer. The zeta potential measurement was performed using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern). 1H NMR spectrum was conducted on a 600 MHz NMR spectrometer (AVANCE NEO, Bruker, Germany) with D2O and DMSO-d6 as the solvent.

U251, U87 cells were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum, penicillin, and streptomycin (100 μg/mL). Cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2. MGA-CDs was dissolved in DMSO (MP Biomedicals, 19605580, California, USA) and diluted to various concentrations with medium. U251 cells were treated with erastin (MedChemExpress, HY-15763, Shanghai, China) diluted to 10 nm/mol in culture medium for 4 h.

Cells were lysed in RIPA buffer (Beyotime, Shanghai, China) for 30 min and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatants were collected. The protein concentration was determined using the BCA method (Thermo Scientific, 23222, USA). The proteins were separated by SDS–PAGE and transferred to a polyvinylidenefluoride membrane (Merck Millipore, ISEQ00010, USA). The PVDF membrane was incubated for 1 h in 5 % skim milk powder at room temperature and then incubated with the primary antibodies overnight at 4 °C (Anti-SLC7A11, Abcam, ab175186, Massachusetts, US; anti-COX2, Abcam, ab179800, Massachusetts, US; anti-GPX4, Abcam, ab125066, US; anti-TfR, Abcam, ab214039, US; anti-PLPP4, GeneTex, GTX85224). After washing thrice for 10 min with PBST, the membrane was incubated with the secondary antibody at 37 °C for 1 h (GAPDH, Proteintech, 60004-1-Ig, Wuhan, China). The protein bands were visualized using enhanced chemiluminescence reagents (Abbkine, Wuhan, China, BMU102-CN). The ChemiDoc imaging system (Bio– Rad, USA) was used to capture the images and quantify the intensity of the protein fragments.

Total RNA was extracted from cells using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen, CA, USA), and its concentration and purity were determined using a Nanodrop2000 microultraviolet spectrophotometer. Total RNA was reverse transcribed to synthesize cDNA using PrimeScript RT reagent kit (Perfect Real Time; TaKaRa) and subjected to qRT-PCR analysis on an Applied Biosystems 7500 fast real-time PCR system (Roche; software version 2.0.5) with SYBR Green qPCR SMix (ROX; Roche). The gene-specific primers used are provided in the online Supplementary Table 1.

U251 and U87 cells (1 × 106 cell/ml, FBS-free) were added to the transwell chamber covered with Matrigel (Corning, 256234, USA), and 500 μl medium containing 10 % FBS was added to the 24-well subplate chamber. The transwell chamber was removed after 18 h of culture and fixed with 4 % formaldehyde for 30 min. The cells were stained with 0.1 % crystal violet and washed thrice with PBS. Five microscope fields were photographed for each group, and the cell numbers were counted using ImageJ software.

Cells were inoculated into a 6-well plate and transfected for 48 h. A 2 mm width scratch was made in the middle of the tissue culture plate and cultured for another 48 h. Photographs were taken at certain time points, and the scratch healing rate was calculated using ImageJ software.

Total RNA was extracted using the TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. RNA purity and quantification were evaluated using the NanoDrop 2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). RNA integrity was assessed using the Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Then the libraries were constructed using VAHTS Universal V6 RNA-seq Library Prep Kit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Differential expression analysis was performed using the DESeq2. p value < 0.05 and fold change > 2 or fold change < 0.5 was set as the threshold for significantly differential expression gene (DEGs). Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analysis of the differentially expressed genes were performed by Enrichr analysis. The RNA sequencing and analysis were conducted by OE Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

PLPP4-ShRNA (5′-GCTTGCCATAAACCCTACGTT-3′, 5′-GGAGTGATGAACTCGGAAATG-3′) were synthesized by Genechem (Shanghai, China). PLPP4-ShRNAs were cloned into a phU6-MCS-Ubiquitin-EGFP-IRES-puromycin plasmid (GV280, Genechem, China). Cell transfection was performed using Lipofectamine 3000 (Invitrogen Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

After protein extraction, the protein concentration was determined using the BCA method (Thermo Scientific, 23222, USA). Iron levels in the samples were determined using an iron ion detection kit (Leagene, Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The corresponding reagents were added in turn and mixed gently at 37 °C for 10 min, and the absorbance of the detection well was measured at 562 nm. Finally, the plasma and serum Fe (mM/L) were measured as follows: [Fe] = [A determination-(A serum blank × 0.970)]/A standard × 35.8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of intracellular lipid peroxidation rate (LPO) levels
ce:section.ce:para: To calculate the production of intracellular LPO, a lipid Peroxidation sensor (C11 BODIPY 581/591, MKBIO, Shanghai, China) was used. First, the lipid Peroxidation sensor C11 BODIPY 581/591 was diluted to 50 mM in serum-free culture medium, and 1 ml C11 BODIPY 581/591 working solution was added to each well at 37 °C in the dark for 1 h. Then, the cells were washed with serum-free medium 3 times to fully remove C11 BODIPY 581/591 that did not enter the cells. Finally, the cells were observed under fluorescence microscope and photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of phospholipid and phosphatidic acid
ce:section.ce:para: U251 cells were incubated with MGA-CDs for 24 h, cells were broken by ultrasound, and the supernatants was collected for analysis. The levels of phospholipid and phosphatidic acid were measured via ELISA kits (Shanghai Ruifan Biological Technology) according to the manufacturer’s protocols.
These procedures have been previously described in detail [18].

BEND3 cells were inoculated to the transwell plate (Poluester film) at 1 × 106 cells/well. The culture medium was replaced every day and the transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) test was performed concurrently. When the TEER value reaches ≥ 150 Ω • cm2, indicating that in vitro BBB model is successfully constructed. MGA-CDs and TMZ were added into the top chamber. After 2 h of incubation, the TEER value was analyzed and the medium in the bottom chamber was collected for content measurement via Ultraviolet–visible spectra [19]. TMZ can be monitored by the intensity of the UV/Vis maxima at 330 nm [20]. MGA-CDs exhibits absorption peak at 283 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Penetrability was determined by quantifying the proportion of MGA-CDs that penetrated to the bottom chamber.

All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Central South University. U251-GFP-Luci-Vector cell was collected, resuspended at 5 × 105 cells in 5 μl of serum-free medium per animal, and then stereotactically injected into the striatum (1.0 mm anterior and 1.0 mm lateral from the Bregma suture and 3.0 mm below the pial surface) of nude mice. A total of 15 mice were used for the intracranial xenograft tumor model, five mice per group. TMZ was formulated in 3 % DMSO, 50 % PEG300 and 0.5 % Tween 80 as suggested by Selleck Chemicals. Cells were implanted into brain of nude mice. Tumor growth was measured 40 days after tumor inoculation using an IVIS Lumina III in Vivo Imaging System. Then, mice received tail vein injection of TMZ (400 μg/ml), MGA-CDs (400 μg/ml) and PBS every 2 days for 14 days. At the end of treatment, mice were again subjected to in vivo fluorescence imaging to observe tumor growth, tumor fluorescence intensity was measured using the IVIS Lumina III in vivo imaging system.

Data are presented as the mean ± SD from at least three separate experiments, and the data were analyzed using GraphPad Prism 5. Differences between the variables of the 2 groups were tested using the Student t-test, and one-way ANOVA was used to evaluate the differences among variables of multiple groups. Survival analysis for the xenograft tumor model mice were calculated by the Kaplan–Meier method. Values of P < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: New strategy for development of therapeutic agents in glioblastoma based on carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Metformin, as a commonly drug in clinical practice, has been widely used beyond the diabetes filed, and become a potential anti-tumor drug [21,22]. However, metformin has been limited in glioblastoma due to poor BBB permeability [23]. We try to synthesize a new CDs agent base on metformin. Due to the properties of CDs and enhanced permeability and retention effect, the CDs agent will have positive blood–brain barrier permeability and tumor cell targeting [24]. The hydroxyl-rich citric acid and gallic acid were selected to react with the amino-rich metformin molecule to produce CDs, and named it MCA-CDs (formed by metformin and citric acid), MGA-CDs (formed by metformin and gallic acid) (Fig. 1 a, Supplementary Fig. 1).

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of each CDs to U251 cells and U87 cells, the viability rate of cells was measured by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) after treatment with CDs for 24 h. TMZ, the preferred chemotherapeutic drug for glioblastoma, was selected as positive control. Both U251 and U87 cells were treated with five different concentrations of MCA-CDs, MGA-CDs and TMZ. After 24 h, the cell viability was detected (Fig. 1b-d) and the half inhibitory concentration (IC50) was calculated (Fig. 1e). The IC50 value of MGA-CDs and MCA-CDs to U251 cells were 117.97 μg/ml and 354.449 μg/ml, to U87 cells were 121.38 μg/ml and 470.565 μg/ml, whereas, in control the IC50 of TMZ to U251 cells and U87 cells were 235.18 μg/ml and 212.37 μg/ml respectively (Fig. 1e). Duo to MGA-CDs has higher inhibitory rate for glioblastoma cells than that of TMZ, we chose it for further research. Then, the CCK-8 and EdU (5-Ethynyl-2′-deoxyuridine) results showed that MGA-CDs significantly inhibited the proliferation of glioblastoma cells (Fig. 1f-g). In consideration of invasion characteristic of glioblastoma into normal brain tissue, which is the main reason why it is difficult to treat. We inspected the inhibitory effect of MGA-CDs on cells invasion, and found that MGA-CDs significantly inhibited the migration and invasion of glioblastoma cells (Fig. 1h-i).

To verify the accessibility of MGA-CDs to BBB, BBB model in vitro was established by using the transwell system (Fig. 1j). The serum free medium containing MGA-CDs or TMZ was added to the inset chamber of the transwell system. After incubation for 4 h, the transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) test showed negligible changes throughout the experiment, demonstrating the integrity of the BBB model during the measurement (Fig. 1k). The content determination of culture medium in the basolateral chamber showed the preferable penetration efficiency of MGA-CDs and TMZ, although the penetration efficiency of MGA-CDs was inferior to that of TMZ (Fig. 1l).

In this work, MGA-CDs with excellent antitumor effects was successfully fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal method using metformin and gallic acid as precursors. As shown in the Fig. 2 a, MGA-CDs have good BBB permeability and mitochondrial targeting due to the small size of MGA-CDs and the introduction of positively charged guanidine groups. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of the prepared MGA-CDs showed that they had uniform size and good dispersion with an average particle size of 2 nm (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution TEM of the MGA-CDs showed a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm (Supplementary Fig. 2), attributed to the d-spacing of the graphite (001) plane [25]. The particle size frequency distribution was obtained by random counting of 50 particles in the TEM images using ImageJ software (Fig. 2c), and the average particle size was determined to be 2.0 ± 0.5 nm. The optical properties of MGA-CDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectra. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MGA-CDs, a strong absorption band at 210 nm corresponds to the CO characteristic absorption of carboxylic acids. However, the characteristic peak of CO in the MGA-CDs was weakened, indicating a possible decarboxylation. The UV–vis of gallic acid at 262 nm corresponds to a π-π* transition of electrons on the benzene ring, while a corresponding red shift in absorption occurs in MGA-CDs, suggesting that groups such as –NH2 and –CONH may be introduced into the carbon dots (Fig. 2f). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra reveal that the wavelength of maximal emission intensity was found at 460 nm, while the excitation was at 360 nm (Fig. 2e). Additionally, MGA-CDs exhibits excitation-dependent properties in the range of 340–460 nm. In the case of MGA-CDs, the FL emission spectrum exhibited a distinct peak at 460 nm at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 2d). The photograph of MGA-CDs solution was green under the excitation wavelength at 385 nm, which was consistent with the FL properties shown in Fig. 2d (Fig. 2d inset). In the FTIR MGA-CDs spectrum shown in Fig. 2g, peaks appear at 3366, 3226, 2953, 1613, 1560, 1400 and 1035 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of –OH, –NH, C-H, CO/CC, amide Ⅱ, amide Ⅲ and C-O groups, respectively. Meanwhile, the FTIR spectra of metformin hydrochloride and gallic acid were compared to show that MGA-CDs retained the active functional groups of the precursors.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern observed at about 22°corresponds to the (001) crystal plane of carbon (Fig. 3 a). XPS analysis (Fig. 3b) showed that MGA-CDs consisted of C, N and O atoms with weight percentages of 71.35, 22.35 and 6.30 %, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s may be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of about 284.5, 285.8, and 287.5 eV, which corresponds to C-C/CC, C-N, and CN, respectively (Fig. 3c). In the N1s spectrum, band can be resolved into two peaks at 399.4, and 401.1 eV, representing pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 3d). The O1s band contains three peaks at 530.8, 532.5 and 535.4 eV for OC-N, C-OH/C-O-C, and –COOH, respectively (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the zeta potential of the synthesized MGA-CDs was found to be 3.47 mV (Fig. 3f), which clearly indicates that the surface of the MGA-CDs is completely functionalized by positive charged functional groups (e.g., amino and guanidinium group). In order to further investigate the stability of MGA-CDs in a solution, the MGA-CDs were dispersed, and samples were prepared at 0 h, 10 h, and 30 h, respectively. Upon examination using electron microscopy, slight aggregation of the MGA-CDs was observed (Supplementary Fig. 3). In order to obtain clearer information about the structure of MGA-CDs, 1H NMR spectrum of gallic acid (Supplementary Fig. 5), metformin hydrochloride (Supplementary Fig. 6), and MGA-CDs (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 7) were characterized. There was a signal in the 1H NMR spectrum of MGA-CDs at 1–2 ppm, corresponding to aliphatic hydrogen. peaks between 2 and 4 ppm were probably ascribed to specific functional groups, such as N-H, CH-N, O-H, or the methyl group in metformin. The large resonance at 4.8 ppm was caused by the O-H group, which was most likely from D2O. Finally, peaks at 6–8.5 ppm can be ascribed to hydrogens in aromatics and imines (Fig. 3g). These results demonstrate that the prepared MGA-CDs have a mixed structure including sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MGA-CDs targets mitochondria and induces ferroptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Metformin exerts its biological effects by targeting mitochondria and remodeling cellular metabolic processes [26]. In addition, targeting subcellular structures, especially mitochondria, leading to metabolic disorders is a very effective anticancer effect. So, we used the photoluminescence characteristics of MGA-CDs to make it emit green fluorescence in U251 cells after being excited by laser. By co-localization analysis, we found that MGA-CDs mainly aggregated in mitochondria (Fig. 4 a). Compared with the untreated group, we detected that the shrunken mitochondria and the reduced numbers of mitochondrial crista after glioblastoma cells were treated with MGA-CDs via transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 4b). It is well known that the shrunken mitochondrial and mitochondrial crista shedding are the characteristic of cells ferroptosis [27]. The ferroptosis was driven by an overload of lipid peroxides on cellular membranes dependent on iron [28], our results indicated that MGA-CDs increased the lipid peroxidation rate (LPO) and the levels of divalent iron ions in U251 cells (Fig. 4c-d). Furthermore, we detected the expression of ferroptosis suppressor genes (GPX4 and SLC7A11) and ferroptosis driver genes (COX2 and TfR) by Western blotting. The results showed that MGA-CDs down-regulated the GPX4 and SLC7A11, while increased expression of COX2 and TfR (Fig. 4e). Taken together, these data suggested that MGA-CDs could target mitochondria and potentially promote ferroptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MGA-CDs induces ferroptosis by impacting glycerophospholipid metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: To further insights into the ferroptosis mechanism induced by MGA-CDs, RNA-seq analysis were performed in U251 cells with and without MGA-CDs treatment. Ferroptosis is a unique mode of cell death driven by phospholipid peroxidation of iron dependence, which is regulated by a variety of cellular metabolic pathways, including lipid metabolism [29]. KEGG pathway analysis showed that multiple lipid metabolism pathways were enriched in U251 cells treated with MGA-CDs, involving in 16 differentially expressed genes (Fig. 5 a-b). Next, we used TCGA database to analyze the alteration frequency of all these 16 differential genes in glioblastoma patients (Fig. 5c). RT-qPCR confirmed that 8 genes (ACOX2, DHCR7, FADS2, GDPD1, CEL, FASN, SCD, HMGCS1) were up-regulated and 3 genes (GAL3ST1, AKR1B10, PLPP4) were down-regulated in U251 cells treatment with MGA-CDs compared to without (Fig. 5d). We conducted the expression analysis of three down-regulated genes in TCGA database, and pan carcinoma analysis [30] indicated that only PLPP4 was increased expression in both low grade glioma (LGG) and glioblastoma (GBM) (Supplementary Fig. 4). Meanwhile, combined with literature researches, we found that PLPP4 has not been reported in relationship between lipid metabolism pathways and ferroptosis. And, Western Blot further confirmed that MGA-CDs would down-regulate PLPP4 expression (Fig. 5e). PLPP4 is a key phospholipid phosphatase involved in glycerophospholipid metabolism, which catalyzes the dephosphorization of phosphatidic acid (PA) to form phospholipid (PL), such as lecithin and cephalin. More importantly, it is poorly understood that the relationship between ferroptosis and glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway. Thus, we detected the content of PA and PL in glioblastoma treatment with MGA-CDs. Results demonstrate that MGA-CDs can promote the accumulation of PA and decreases the production of PL (Fig. 5f-g). Collectively, these results demonstrate that MGA-CDs may induce ferroptosis by targeting PLPP4 to impact glycerophospholipid metabolism pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PLPP4 may be a novel ferroptosis suppressor
ce:section.ce:para: To determine whether PLPP4 was required for ferroptosis induced by MGA-CDs, we designed ShRNA targeting PLPP4 to interfere with PLPP4 expression. As verified by RT-qPCR and Western Blot, ShRNA could significantly down-regulate the expression of PLPP4 in U251 cells (Fig. 6a-b). As expected, the downregulation of PLPP4 promoted ferroptosis. As mentioned above, the increase in divalent iron ions and lipid peroxidation rate (LPO) are hallmarks of ferroptosis. By determining the levels of divalent iron ions and LPO, we found that knockdown of PLPP4 in U251 cells treatment with the ferroptosis inducer erastin increase the LPO and the levels of divalent iron ions (Fig. 6c-d). Then, in the case of the ferroptosis inducer erastin treatment, down-regulation of PLPP4 also suppressed GPX4 and SLC7A11 expression but increased COX2 and TfR expression (Fig. 6 b). Moreover, we also compared the expression of PLPP4 with or without Erastin treatment in U251 cell. As shown in Fig. 6e, the expression of PLPP4 is decreased when the ferroptosis of U251 cells was induced by Erastin. Thus, PLPP4 can be considered a novel ferroptosis suppressor gene.

CDs are regarded as a promising medical nanomaterial due to their good biocompatibility and minimal toxicity. Therefore, in order to verify the vivo toxicity of MGA-CDs, we evaluate the potential toxic effects of MGA-CDs by using the complete blood count test, the serum biochemistry assay and histological examination. Using PBS as the solvent, five different concentrations of MGA-CDs solutions (100 μg/ml, 200 μg/ml, 300 μg/ml, 400 μg/ml, 500 μg/ml) were set up and injected into the mice (6 weeks old) respectively. Those mice were treated with 300 μl MGA-CDs solutions or 300 μl PBS by tail vein injection every 2 days for 2 weeks (Supplementary Fig. 8a). There was no significant effect on the weight of all mice during the experiment (Supplementary Fig. 8b). However, compared with PBS injection group, White blood cells (WBC), Platelets (PLT) and Creatinine (CREA) indexes of mice from MGA-CDs injection group with a concentration of 500 ug/ml increased, suggesting that infection and kidney damage may occur (Supplementary Fig. 8c-j). Moreover, the histological examination of H&E-stained slices of the main organs collected from mice treated with MGA-CDs (400 ug/ml) showed did no histological injury (Supplementary Fig. 8k), further demonstrating that MGA-CDs at dose 400 ug/ml would not induce obvious adverse events. Therefore, we determined that the therapeutic concentration of MGA-CDs was 400 ug/ml. Then, we further explored the retention time and metabolic processes of MGA-CDs in vivo. Mice were received tail vein injections of MGA-CDs (300 µl, 400 µg/ml) and PBS (300 µl), urine and feces were collected at regular intervals from mice, and MGA-CDs in urine and feces were quantified by fluorescence intensity. We detected that MGA-CDs can be rapidly metabolized through renal clearance. 8 h after intravenous administration, 86.5 % of MGA-CDs were detected in urine and 13.5 % were detected in feces (Supplementary Fig. 9b-c). And the content of MGA-CDs in blood also decreased over time. 32 h after intravenous administration, MGA-CDs was basically completely cleared from the circulating blood (Supplementary Fig. 9d). Then, the ex vivo imaging of isolated brain confirmed that MGA-CDs can effectively penetrate BBB into brain. 4 h after intravenous injection, the intracranial MGA-CDs content was the highest, and then gradually decreased. 32 h after intravenous injection, MGA-CDs were basically cleared from the intracranial space (Supplementary Fig. 9e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MGA-CDs is a potential therapeutic agent for glioblastoma
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the feasibility of MGA-CDs in glioblastoma treatment, we tested the therapeutic efficiency of MGA-CDs in tumor xenografts in vivo (Fig. 7 a). U251-Luci-GFP-Vector cells were injected into the brain of nude mice, and 40 days later, intracranial tumorigenesis was detected by Vivo Imaging System. Then tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 3 groups with 5 animals in each group, MGA-CDs (400 µg/ml, 300 µl), PBS (300 µl) and TMZ (400 µg/ml, 300 µl) was injected into body by the tail vein of the mouse separately every 2 days for 14 days. As brightly observed under an in Vivo Imaging System, it was shown that both mice treated with TMZ and MGA-CDs showed a significant decrease in fluorescence signal in comparison with mice treated with PBS (Fig. 7b). The relative fluorescence quantification is shown in Fig. 7c. Furthermore, the ex vivo imaging of main organs from tumor-bearing mice was carried out at 2 h after MGA-CDs injection (Fig. 7d). It was shown that MGA-CDs is obviously aggregated in brain tissue, demonstrating the excellent blood brain barrier permeability and brain tumor targeting ability of MGA-CDs.

Next, we conducted H&E staining and the expression detection of PLPR4, COX2, TfR and Ki67 by immunohistochemical staining in intracranial in situ brain tumor tissues. We found that MGA-CDs treatment results in shrinked tumor tissues (HE staining), decreased Ki67 expression, a proliferative index, and increased expression of ferroptosis driver molecule COX2 and TfR (Fig. 7e). Unfortunately, due to PLPP4 antibody (GeneTex, GTX85224), we did not successfully detect the expression of PLPP4 in xenograft tumor tissues, but RT-qPCR detection validated that MGA-CDs suppressed the expression of PLPP4 (Fig. 7f). These results indicated that MGA-CDs inhibited tumor growth and induced tumor cells ferroptosis in vivo.

Here we successfully prepared a novel CDs (MGA-CDs) base on metformin and natural phytochemical gallic acid via one-pot hydrothermal method, and demonstrated that MGA-CDs can significantly inhibit glioblastoma growth. MGA-CDs have two unique and significant advantages over other carbon dots-based GBM therapy. First, MGA-CDs, similar to previously reported CDs, can freely penetrate the blood–brain barrier without the aid of any extra targeting molecules. BBB is the greatest impediment for brain tumor treatment, which hinder therapeutic drugs deliver into the CNS from blood circulation system. As an emerging nanomaterial, CDs has demonstrated promising applications in the drug delivery field, especially in term of BBB penetration [31,32]. Zheng et al developed carbon dots (CD-Asp) via a straight forward pyrolysis route by using D-glucose and L-aspartic acid as precursor. CD-Asp can freely penetrate the BBB and precisely targeting glioma [33]. Similarly, Cilingir et al. have also reported a new metformin derived carbon dots (Met-CDs). Met-CDs was prepared by microwave assisted method using metformin and Citric acid as precursor. Met-CDs as a fluorescent nanomaterial characterized by high biocompatibility and BBB permeability, which are outstanding candidates for living cell bio-imaging studies [34]. However, it should be notice that Met-CDs and CD-Asp are mainly used for biological imaging of tumors, both of which lack biological toxicity to tumor cells. Second, MGA-CDs are capable of targeted tumors with a high degree of specificity and efficiency, and exerts significant anticancer effects in vivo. In previous studies, researchers have mostly focused on the function carbon dots as drug carriers. For example, Li et al synthesized LAAM TC-CQDs by amino-rich1,4,5,8-tetraminoanthraquinone with hydroxyl-rich citric acid. LAAM TC-CQDs, as a drug carrier, can deliver DNA-damaging chemotherapy drugs target to brain tumor [16]. Then, the tumor-targeting efficiency of CDs can be improved through the conjugation of tumor-targeting ligands. Hettiarachchi et al conjugate carbon dots with transferrin, epirubicin and temozolomide to build the triple conjugated system [35]. Because transferrin receptor is overexpressed on tumor cells and endothelial cells, the triple conjugated system can smoothly penetrate BBB via receptor-mediated transcytosis and target tumor cells [36]. Not come singly but in pairs, Liu et al also synthesized iron-doped orange emissive carbon dots and coupled with transferrin and glucose oxidase, named it IGTCDs. IGTCDs not only have ideal biocompatibility, but also is provided with efficient glioma targeted effect [37]. MGA-CDs do not require any additional modifications to target tumor cells and accumulate in mitochondria. Most importantly, MGA-CDs are themselves antineoplastic agents, which inhibits glioblastoma growth by inducing ferroptosis.

In our study, we chose two hydroxy-rich ligands to prepare amino-rich metformin molecules into carbon dots, namely citric acid and gallic acid. The results show that carbon dots generated with gallic acid as ligand has stronger antitumor activity. Gallic acid (GA, also named 3,4,5-trihydroxybenzoic acid), as a natural phytochemical, widely exists in in various plants including witch hazel, sumac, gallnuts, oak bark, tea leaves [38]. GA content three hydroxyl groups in its structure, it provides a chemical basis for modifying the structure of GA (such as preparing carbon dots) to improve its anti-tumor activity. GA has been characterized with huge numbers of biological activities, including anti-inflammatory [39], anti-oxidant [40], anti-tumor [41], hepatoprotective [42], nephroprotective [43], neuroprotective [44]. Especially, the anti-tumor activity of GA has been deeply investigated in numerous experiments. Similar to metformin, although GA has good anti-tumor properties, its efficacy may be limited by some factors (including bioavailability, low stability and absorption). To address these shortcomings, several studies have established that nanoparticles can enhance the bioavailability of GA by promoting the internalization and accumulation of drug molecules in cancer cells [45,46]. In this study, the MGA-CDs formed by metformin and gallic acid after a series of dehydration and decarboxylation reactions can enhance the anticancer effect.

The mechanism of BBB penetration mainly is divided into passive and active routes. Small, amphiphilicity and low charge molecules are generally penetrating the BBB by passive diffusion. Conversely, large, hydrophilic and highly charged molecules overcome the BBB by active routes, such as carrier- and receptor-mediated transcytosis. Due to advantageous surface properties of CDs, passive diffusion is regarded as the mainstream mechanism of the BBB penetration [47]. It is reported that the tight junctions between endothelial cells have a gap of 4–6 nm [48]. The particles smaller than 4 nm are likely to cross the BBB by passive diffusion. Then, anionic areas existed in tight junctions can attract more cationic CDs to gather in tight junctions, further promote CDs to penetrate the BBB [49]. In this study, the particle size of MGA-CDs is only 2 nm, which is smaller than the gap size of tight junctions. And the guanidine moiety with cations was introduced into MGA-CDs, giving MGA-CDs positive charge. Thus, these advantageous surface properties of MGA-CDs making it easier to be attracted anionic areas existed in tight junctions, and penetrate the BBB by passive diffusion. In addition, because of uncontrolled proliferation, tumor cells are forced to take up more nutrients (e.g., amino acids, glucose) from the environment to fuel biosynthetic processes [50]. It should be noticed that CDs similar to amino acid structures can be specifically taken up by tumor cells, such as LAAM TC-CQDs [16]. The amino and carboxyl surface functional groups of MGA-CDs also hare highly similar to amino acid structures. Hence, tumor cells uptake more MGA-CDs than non-tumor cells due to this structural resemblance between MGA-CDs and amino acid. Further, the positively charged guanidine group endows MGA-CDs with mitochondrial targeting. It should be noticed that due to the negative membrane potential of the mitochondrial inner membrane, positively charged compounds can accumulate in the mitochondrial [51]. When MGA-CDs enter an acidic microenvironment (e.g., tumor microenvironment), the amino groups on their surface can be protonated and endow MGA-CDs more positive charges, enabling it easier to target mitochondria. So, the MGA-CDs not only exert pharmacological activity, but also can target mitochondria.

Ferroptosis is a pharmacological mechanism of antitumor drugs. In particular, ferroptosis is characterized by mitochondrial membrane condensation and reduced mitochondrial ridges. Here, our findings demonstrate that MGA-CDs can target mitochondria and cause ferroptosis-like morphological changes. In 2022, LU et al developed a carbon dots (CHA-CDs) with GSH oxidase-like activities based on chlorogenic acid (ChA), which can also induce ferroptosis in tumor cells [52]. Unlike with CHA-CDs, MGA-CDs induces ferroptosis mainly by affecting lipid metabolism. Most importantly, we screened the lipid metabolism related differential genes caused by MGA-CDs, identified a novel ferroptosis-related gene PLPP4, and identified PLPP4 as a novel ferroptosis suppressor gene.

PLPP4 is a member of phospholipid phosphatase superfamily, which involves in Glycerophospholipid metabolism via catalyzing the dephosphorylation of phosphatidic acid (PA). PLPP4 has been reported to be closely associated with a variety of tumors. PLPP4 is regarded as a stemness-related gene in Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [53]. Coincidentally, PLPP4 is also identified as a potential biomarker associated with EMT in a single-cell RNA-seq data analysis about prostate cancer [54]. Moreover, PLPP4 is regarded as a potential clinical marker and immunotherapeutic target in the glioma by analyzing the expression levels of phospholipid phosphatase family proteins in tumor RNA expression profile datasets from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) [55]. In our study, PLPP4 function as a target of MGA-CDs. MGA-CDs induce ferroptosis by interfering with PLPP4 activity. More importantly, we report for the first time that PLPP4 may be a novel ferroptosis suppressor.

A growing body of evidence has supported that CDs may function as nanozyme. Nanozyme is defined as nanomaterials possess intrinsic enzyme-like properties and is regarded as ideal candidates for disease diagnosis and treatment [56]. The various functional groups present on the surface of CDs to endow with the chemical basis that CDs display enzymatic catalytic activity. For example, CDs can mimic the structure and function of GSH oxidase, catalase, or superoxide dismutase, which leads to oxygen reactive species (ROS) production and subsequent oxidative stress damage in biological systems [52]. The CDs-based nanozymes have already been regarded as a potential antitumor strategy. In future studies, we will also focus on whether MGA-CDs also possess the properties of nanozymes.

In summary, we synthesize a novel glioblastoma therapeutic carbon dots using metformin and gallic acid. MGA-CDs effectively penetrates the BBB, accumulates in tumor cell mitochondria, further inhibits the activity of PLPP4, finally induces ferroptosis. Furthermore, MGA-CDs exerts significant anticancer effects in vivo, which is a potential intracranial tumor therapeutic agent. Particularly, we have first reported that PLPP4 functions as a novel function suppressor gene. This work also presents a viable strategy that development of CDs-based novel therapeutic agent for glioblastoma.

Minghua Wu and Yang Wang conceived and designed the experiments. Kun Deng performed in vitro and animal experiments. Lianglin Zhang synthesized the MGA-CDs and performed MGA-CDs characterization. Kun Deng and Lianglin Zhang performed data analysis. Wei Gao, Xiang Lin and Xinmiao Long performed animal experiments. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.
